talk_metadata.institution_or_pharma: Monell Chemical Senses Center / University of Pennsylvania
talk_metadata.talk_title: Chemosensory dysfunction in cancer: prevalence, impact and unmet needs
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "Currently, we don't have pharmacological solutions to reverse the smell dysfunction or taste dysfunction that really works."
Speaker said: "We don't have good data in the EMR either, because in time‑pressured environments it is often deprioritized."
Speaker said: "There is no wonder drug here... the best we can do right now is nutrition counseling and self‑management strategies."

vulnerability_identified.mechanistic_blindspot: lack of mechanistic understanding of how cancer therapies cause persistent taste and smell loss

key_data_gaps: Limited longitudinal data on recovery of chemosensory function after therapy

crispro_opportunity.description: Absence of validated biomarkers to identify patients at highest risk for chemosensory dysfunction, limiting targeted preventive or therapeutic interventions.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker noted that "we don't have good data in the EMR" and that clinicians often miss the symptom unless specifically asked.
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can integrate multi‑omic and clinical data to discover predictive signatures of chemosensory toxicity for specific regimens.

crispro_opportunity.description: Develop computational models to predict off‑target chemosensory effects of emerging targeted therapies before clinical rollout.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker highlighted that many targeted agents cause taste/smell changes without a clear mechanistic basis.
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO's spine can simulate drug‑target interaction networks to flag potential sensory side‑effects early.

unresolved_questions: What molecular pathways mediate long‑term taste and smell loss after chemotherapy or radiation?
Can early patient‑reported outcome screening improve nutritional outcomes?

talk_metadata.talk_title: Taste bud homeostasis, cancer therapeutics and organoid models
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "We don't fully understand why a steady rundown of taste buds causes dysgeusia so quickly in patients."
Speaker said: "We still lack in vitro models for human taste epithelium."
Speaker said: "The smoothened antagonist knocks out taste buds, yet it is highly effective against cancer – a paradoxical effect."

vulnerability_identified.mechanistic_blindspot: off‑target impact on taste progenitor proliferation leading to excess type‑I cells and loss of sweet perception
inhibition of progenitor differentiation into taste cells without affecting proliferation
vulnerability_identified.hidden_tox_signal: Speaker noted loss of taste buds is a major cause of non‑compliance with these drugs.

vulnerability_identified.mechanistic_blindspot: off‑target inhibition of KIT in taste progenitors shifts fate from sweet to bitter cells

competitive_moat_weakness.vulnerable_ip_or_chemistry: KIT inhibition off‑target in taste epithelium
competitive_moat_weakness.ip_vulnerability_note: Speaker revealed that TKIs designed for VEGF/PDGF pathways unintentionally inhibit KIT in taste buds, causing sweet‑cell loss.
competitive_moat_weakness.clinical_strategy_weakness: Broad use of anti‑angiogenic TKIs without biomarker‑selected dosing leads to pervasive dysgeusia.

cognitive_dissonance: Speaker showed that smoothened antagonists efficiently shrink tumors, then concluded that these drugs cause severe loss of taste buds – a contradiction between tumor efficacy and taste toxicity.

key_data_gaps: No human taste organoid data yet
Lack of clinical correlation between mouse taste phenotypes and patient dysgeusia

crispro_opportunity.description: Identify and model off‑target KIT inhibition in taste progenitors to design selective TKIs that spare taste function.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker described how cabozantinib reduces sweet cells via KIT inhibition.
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can predict off‑target kinase interactions and suggest scaffold modifications to retain anti‑angiogenic activity while preserving KIT signaling in taste.

crispro_opportunity.description: Incorporate chemosensory endpoints (e.g., taste‑bud imaging, patient‑reported dysgeusia) into early‑phase trials of Wnt or hedgehog inhibitors.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker noted that dysgeusia is a major cause of non‑compliance for these agents.
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can simulate patient‑level toxicity profiles to optimize dosing schedules that minimize taste loss.

crispro_opportunity.description: Leverage human taste organoid platform to screen candidate drugs for sensory toxicity before clinical testing.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker emphasized the lack of human taste organoid models and the need for such platforms.
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can integrate organoid assay readouts with computational predictions to prioritize low‑toxicity compounds.

cited_competitors.name: Novartis smoothened antagonist
cited_competitors.context: used clinically for basal cell carcinoma, shown to cause loss of taste buds in mice

cited_competitors.name: Cabozantinib
cited_competitors.context: anti‑angiogenic TKI causing sweet‑cell loss via KIT inhibition

unresolved_questions: Do human taste buds exhibit the same Wnt‑β‑catenin and hedgehog dependencies observed in mice?
Can taste organoids predict patient‑specific dysgeusia risk for new targeted agents?
